kanji.meaningMnemonic: <radical>Writing</radical> and <radical>measurement</radical> stand <kanji>versus</kanji> one another. Writing is all about feeling and expression. Measurement is about cold hard numbers. Yep, in this world writing people are basically the <kanji>opposite</kanji> of measurement people. Which side are you on?
kanji.meaningHint: What's that, you say? It doesn't always have to be writing types versus measurement types? We can work together to create a better society? Nah, sorry, I'd rather it be me versus the world.
kanji.readingMnemonic: Whenever it's you <kanji>versus</kanji> someone else, you wear a <reading>tie</reading> (たい). Naturally, your tie is always the <kanji>opposite</kanji> color of your opponent's tie. Kinda like how political rivals wear different color ties when they debate. It's pretty silly, but at least your tie makes it clear that it's you versus them.
kanji.readingHint: Think of someone you know who's your total opposite. Now imagine you're both in ties, but opposite colors, and you're debating… or fighting in a cage match or something.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: This word is made up of a single kanji with hiragana attached to it. That hiragana ends with い which means it's an adjective. So, what's the adjective form of the kanji that's in this word? 円 means both "yen" and "round" but in this case we're looking at the round one (because something isn't going to be "yenny"). The adjective version of round is <vocabulary>round</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>circular</vocabulary>. Just like a yen coin, this "round" is a flat round. As in, if you draw a circle on a piece of paper, you could use this word. Same goes with yen, which is why "yen" and "round" both use the same kanji.

So, if you know that the reading of 丸 is まる then you'll also know that the reading for this word is <reading>まる</reading>!

vocabulary.readingMnemonic:
Since this word is made up of a single kanji, it uses the kun'yomi reading. 間 has two common kun'yomi readings, あいだ and ま. You haven't learned these readings yet, so here's an explanation of each reading and mnemonics to help you remember them:

間 is usually read as あいだ for a longer interval of time or general space between things. It's read as ま for a short burst of time or when defining a specific area, like a 茶の間 (the older name for a Japanese living room).

You can remember this because time feels longer in <reading>Ida</reading>ho (あいだ). Any interval of time you spend there feels so long, because Idaho life moves so slowly. As soon as you cross the border and enter a new state, you call your <reading>ma</reading> (ま) to tell her you're finally out of that slooooow state, and the intervals of time feel shorter.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
When you stick this onto the end of another word that talks about an <kanji>interval of time</kanji> in some way, you're indicating the <vocabulary>amount of time</vocabulary> that has gone past. For example, 二年間 is "two years." 一時間 is "one hour." Any amount of time + 〜間 will make it an "amount of time" instead of just a specific time.

Note that 〜間 can be omitted, especially in conversations, when it's clear that you're talking about a duration of time from the context. However, the exception is 時. You always say 〜時間 to refer to the amount of hours, to differentiate from 〜時 (〜o'clock).

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
You know that 思う means "to think" and 出す means "to take out." So this is what happens when your brain takes a moment to think and pulls a memory out of storage. It means <vocabulary>to remember</vocabulary>!

You can shout 思い出した！when you recall your keys are in the fridge. (Again?!) You can also say 母を思い出します when something reminds you of your mom. In Japanese, you usually omit what reminded you of the memory because the important thing is the memory itself, filling your heart with touchy-feelies. Oh, mama!

If we're talking about when something happened, the "before" meaning kicks in, and it means <vocabulary>the other day</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>recently</vocabulary>. If we're talking about location, then the "front" meaning kicks in, and it means <vocabulary>in front of this</vocabulary>.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: The reading for 前 is the kun'yomi, like the reading for the vocabulary word 前. So long as you know that word, you'll be able to read this one too. The reason it's not on'yomi is because it's like two words put together. The word この and the word 前.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>change</kanji> so you'd think the verb version is "to change," but it's a bit more specific. This word means <vocabulary>to change form</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to transform</vocabulary>.

化ける means to change into something else, usually in a surprising and magical way. It's often used in stories where animals or spirits take on human forms. It can also describe transformations in real life that are so dramatic they feel unreal.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic:
Since this word consists of a kanji with hiragana attached, you can bet that it will use the kun'yomi reading. You didn't learn that reading with this kanji, so here's a mnemonic to help you: 

You try to transform. You want to turn into something awesome, like a dinosaur, but then *poof* you turn into a sheep. "<reading>Ba</reading>aaa," you say. "Baaa."

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
You've already learned 回る, which means "to revolve" or "to rotate," and this is the transitive version of that. You're doing the turning/rotating action on something else, so this means <vocabulary>to turn something</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>to rotate something</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>to spin something</vocabulary>.

To remember the difference, just think about how you might get <reading>sue</reading>d (す) if you started aggressively spinning one of those playground spinners while someone else's kids were playing on it. Pretty shady behavior.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: The reading is the same as the word 回る (まわ). So long as you know that word, you'll be able to read this one as well. Use both of these words together to remember their readings.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
<kanji>I</kanji> am talking about my <kanji>self</kanji>, my own <kanji>body</kanji>… In other words, this is about me <vocabulary>personally</vocabulary> or what I <vocabulary>myself</vocabulary> have done.

私自身 literally means "I myself," and it's used to emphasize your personal involvement in something. For example, you may say 私自身の考え to highlight that something is your own thought, or 私自身から言う, to really stress that you'll personally be the one to say something to someone, without going through any intermediaries.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: The readings are a bit weird here. The 私 is わたし, but the 自 and 身 use the readings you learned with the kanji. Just imagine the 私 saying to the other two, "I myself will use my vocab reading, but you two should use your kanji readings — got it?"
